v.s: Li Bai, the great romantic poet of the Tang dynasty, was born in Suyab (near modern-day Tokmok, Kyrgyzstan). Li Bai, courtesy name Taibai, art name Qinglian Jushi, moved with his father to Changlong, Mianzhou (modern Jiangyou, Sichuan) at age five. He wrote a large number of popular poems in a bold, unrestrained and imaginative style, and was revered as the 'Immortal Poet'. His representative works include 'Quiet Night Thought', 'The Waterfall in Mount Lu Viewed from Afar', and 'Bringing in the Wine'. His poetry had a profound influence on Chinese literature.

During her reign, Wu Zetian vigorously reformed the imperial examination system, pioneering the palace examination and military examination. In 701, she further expanded the scope of the examination to allow children of common and humble families to enter officialdom through exams, breaking the monopoly of aristocratic clans over official selection. This reform brought many talented people to the Tang court in the mid-to-late period, promoted social mobility, and reserved wise chancellors such as Yao Chong and Song Jing for the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

After unifying the Three Kingdoms, the Silla dynasty on the Korean Peninsula entered a golden age of culture. Around 701, Silla achieved outstanding accomplishments in Buddhist art, metalwork, and architecture. Bulguksa Temple and Dabotap Pagoda in Gyeongju represented the highest level of East Asian Buddhist architecture at the time. Silla monks traveled extensively to Tang China to study Buddhism and introduced Chinese Buddhist scriptures and Tang cultural systems to the peninsula, promoting cultural exchange in Northeast Asia.

In 702, Wu Zetian decreed the addition of a military examination to the imperial examination system to select military talent. The military exam tested mounted archery, foot archery, level shooting, weightlifting, and负重, and was administered by the Ministry of War. This system provided a path for humble families to advance through military service, producing renowned generals such as Guo Ziyi. The military examination was followed by later dynasties until the late Qing.

v.t: Promulgation of the Taihō Code in Japan
v.s: In 702, Emperor Monmu of Japan promulgated the Taihō Code, the first complete written law code of Japan. Based on the Tang 'Yonghui Code' and adapted to Japanese conditions, it consisted of penal law (ritsu) and administrative law (ryō). The establishment of the ritsuryō system marked Japan's formal transition to a centralized, legal-code state, promoting standardization of land systems, official ranks, and household registration.

v.t: Beginning of the Translation Movement in the Arab Empire
v.s: In the early 8th century, under the Umayyad Caliphate, the systematic translation of Greek, Persian, and Indian scientific works began. In Damascus and Alexandria, scholars translated works of Aristotle, Euclid, Ptolemy, and Galen into Arabic. The first Arabic scholarly works in astronomy, medicine, mathematics, and alchemy appeared, laying the foundation for the translation movement at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad during the Abbasid Caliphate.

v.t: Arab Conquest of Central Asian States
v.s: Umayyad general Qutayba ibn Muslim led forces to attack the Transoxiana region, conquering the city-states of Bukhara, Samarkand, and Khwarazm. The Arab army used cavalry superiority and exploited rivalries among city-states to force local rulers to submit and pay tribute. Islam began to spread in Central Asia, while Buddhism and Zoroastrianism were suppressed. This conquest extended the Arab Empire's borders to the Amu Darya river, bringing them into direct contact with Tang China's sphere of influence.

In 703, Wu Zetian sent envoys to Tibet to negotiate marriage alliance. The Tibetan king Tridu Songtsen sent a return mission, and the two sides reached preliminary agreements on border conflicts and trade. This diplomatic interaction temporarily eased tensions between Tang and Tibet over Qinghai and the Western Regions, paving the way for the later heqin (marriage alliance) of Princess Jincheng.

In the early 8th century, Chang'an had a population of over one million, making it one of the largest cities in the world. Merchants, envoys, and monks from the Western Regions, Central Asia, Persia, and Arabia lived in the city, and the Western Market was crowded with Hu (non-Han) merchants. Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, Zoroastrianism, Manichaeism, and Islam coexisted in Chang'an, and Hu clothing, music, and food became popular in Tang society. This open and inclusive international cultural atmosphere had a profound impact on East Asian civilization.

In the early 8th century, the Vikings of Scandinavia began to expand overseas. They built fast, shallow-draft longships that could sail on the open sea as well as up rivers. Early Vikings engaged primarily in trade and raiding, attacking the British Isles and the coasts of Francia. Starting with the raid on Lindisfarne Priory in 793, the Viking Age officially began, with influence reaching modern-day Britain, Ireland, France, Russia, and Italy.

v.t: Political Turmoil in Wu Zetian's Late Reign
v.s: In 704, Wu Zetian was advanced in age. The Zhang Yizhi and Zhang Changzong brothers controlled the court, causing discontent among officials. Chancellor Zhang Jianzhi and others plotted to restore the Tang dynasty, laying the groundwork for the Shenlong Coup the following year. Although Wu Zetian's later reign saw political corruption, she had stabilized the frontiers, developed the economy, and improved the imperial examination system during her time in power, setting the stage for the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

In the first month of 705, Chancellor Zhang Jianzhi, Cui Xuanwei, and others, together with Right Feather Forest General Li Duozuo, launched a coup, leading the Forest Army into Wu Zetian's bedchamber and killing the Zhang brothers. Wu Zetian was forced to abdicate and pass the throne to Crown Prince Li Xian. Emperor Zhongzong of Tang was restored, and the dynasty's name reverted to Tang, with the capital moved back to Chang'an. The Shenlong Coup ended Wu Zetian's rule, and the Li clan regained power.

In December 705, Wu Zetian died of illness at the Shangyang Palace in Luoyang at the age of 82. In her final edict, she renounced the title of emperor and was called 'Holy Empress Zetian', and was buried with Emperor Gaozong of Tang in the Qianling Mausoleum. Wu Zetian was the only orthodox female emperor in Chinese history. She ruled for nearly half a century, promoting the imperial examinations, developing the economy, and consolidating the frontiers, laying the foundation for the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

v.t: Formation of Arab Silk Trade Network
v.s: In the early 8th century, after the Arab Empire took control of the middle section of the Silk Road, silk trade flourished. Persian brocades, Syrian embroidery, and Chinese silk were traded in the bazaars of Baghdad, Damascus, and Cairo. Silk was not only a luxury good but also used as currency and diplomatic gifts. Arab merchants sold silk as far as Europe, East Africa, and Southeast Asia via land and sea routes, establishing a commercial network covering most of the Old World.

In 706, Emperor Zhongzong ordered the full restoration of Tang systems, abolishing special offices and positions established during the Wu Zhou reign. Official titles were reverted to old names, and the operation of the three departments and six ministries was re-established. Chang'an was restored as the capital, with Luoyang as the eastern capital. These measures marked the restoration of the Li clan's legitimacy, but also restored Zhongzong's inept governance, with Empress Wei and Princess Anle interfering in court affairs.

In 706, Japanese envoys to Tang China brought back Nestorian Christian scriptures from Chang'an. Although Nestorianism did not spread widely in Japan, this cultural exchange reflects the openness of East Asian states to Tang culture in the early 8th century. Nestorianism had legal status in Tang China, with a Persian temple in Chang'an's Yining Ward.

In July 707, Crown Prince Li Chongjun, unable to bear suppression by Empress Wei and Wu Sansi, staged a coup together with Forest Army general Li Duozuo. They killed Wu Sansi and his son and then attacked the imperial palace, attempting to kill Empress Wei and Princess Anle. Emperor Zhongzong called out to the soldiers from the city tower, and they turned against Li Chongjun. Li Chongjun was killed while fleeing after his defeat. This rebellion exposed the intense power struggles within the imperial family under Zhongzong, and Empress Wei's influence grew further.

In the early 8th century, the English missionary Saint Boniface conducted large-scale evangelization in Germania. He cut down the sacred oak of Geismar, a tree sacred to the Germanic people who worshipped the god Thor, to prove the power of the Christian god over the pagan gods. Boniface established several bishoprics in Germany, reformed church discipline, and became the 'Apostle to the Germans', laying the foundation for cooperation between the Carolingian dynasty and the Church.

In 708, Tibetan forces raided the Longyou region. Tang general Guo Yuanzhen led the army to counterattack. The Tang army decisively defeated the Tibetans at Tao Prefecture (modern Lintan, Gansu), killing thousands and capturing large amounts of livestock and supplies. This victory consolidated Tang's western frontier and deterred Tibetan incursions for a time. Guo Yuanzhen then set up beacon towers and garrison farms to strengthen the defensive system.

v.t: Arab Empire Advances into Central Asia
v.s: In 708, Umayyad general Qutayba ibn Muslim crossed the Amu Darya and attacked Ferghana and Samarkand. The Arab army adopted a strategy of divide and rule, allying with some Sogdian city-states to fight against Tang's vassals in the Western Regions. By 711, the Arabs had basically controlled the Transoxiana region, and Islam began to spread into the interior of Central Asia.

In 709, Emperor Zhongzong gave Princess Jincheng, daughter of Li Shouli, Prince of Yong, in marriage to the Tibetan king Tri Detsuktsen. The emperor personally escorted the princess to Shiping County (modern Xingping, Shaanxi) and hosted a banquet for Tibetan envoys. The marriage improved Tang-Tibetan relations, and both sides agreed to set the Chiling (modern Riyue Mountain, Qinghai) as the border and conduct mutual trade. Princess Jincheng lived in Tibet for nearly thirty years and promoted cultural exchange between Tang and Tibet.

v.t: Arab Army Reaches the Indus River
v.s: In 709, Umayyad general Muhammad ibn al-Qasim led forces to attack the Indus Valley. The Arabs captured the important city of Debal in Sindh, defeating the local Hindu ruler. By 712, the Arabs had fully conquered Sindh, bringing Islam into the Indian subcontinent. The Arabs established strongholds along the Indus River as a base for further eastward expansion.

In June 710, Emperor Zhongzong was poisoned to death by Empress Wei and Princess Anle. Empress Wei installed Li Chongmao as emperor and took the reins of government, preparing to imitate Wu Zetian's rise to power. Prince Linzi, Li Longji, together with Princess Taiping and Forest Army generals, launched a coup, killing Empress Wei, Princess Anle, and their faction, and supporting his father, Prince Xiang Li Dan, as emperor (Emperor Ruizong). Li Longji was made crown prince for his merits and effectively held power.

v.t: Empress Genmei Moves Capital to Nara
v.s: In 710, Empress Genmei of Japan moved the capital from Fujiwara-kyō to Heijō-kyō (modern Nara), beginning the Nara period. Heijō-kyō was designed as a grid pattern imitating Tang Chang'an, about 4.3 km east-west and 4.8 km north-south. After the move, Japan fully absorbed Tang systems and culture, the ritsuryō system was further refined, Buddhism gained state protection, and culture and arts flourished.

v.t: Beginning of Iconoclasm in the Byzantine Empire
v.s: In the early 8th century, Byzantine Emperor Leo III began to promote iconoclastic policies. He believed that the veneration of icons violated the commandment against idolatry and ordered the removal of icons and frescoes from churches. This policy met strong opposition from monasteries and the Pope, leading to a century-long iconoclastic controversy that deepened the schism between Eastern and Western Christianity.

In 711, the Umayyad Berber general Tariq ibn Ziyad led about 7,000 troops across the Strait of Gibraltar into the Iberian Peninsula. Visigothic King Roderic met them in battle and was defeated and killed at the Battle of Guadalete. Tariq quickly captured Córdoba, Toledo, and other cities. By 718, Muslims controlled most of the Iberian Peninsula and established the province of Al-Andalus with its center at Córdoba. Islamic civilization lasted more than 700 years in Spain, leaving behind magnificent mosques and advanced scholarly traditions.

In 711, during the reign of Emperor Ruizong, Princess Taiping's influence grew due to her role in his ascension. She promoted her own followers extensively; five of the seven chancellors were her protégés. Her conflict with Crown Prince Li Longji intensified. Princess Taiping attempted to depose Li Longji and install a more pliable crown prince. Ruizong tried to mediate but failed, eventually abdicating in favor of Li Longji in 712.

In August 712, Emperor Ruizong abdicated in favor of Crown Prince Li Longji, who ascended the throne at Taiji Palace in Chang'an as Emperor Xuanzong of Tang. He was 28 years old. He worked diligently, appointing Yao Chong and Song Jing as chancellors, reforming officialdom, developing the economy, and reforming the military, beginning a 44-year reign. In the early part of his reign, Xuanzong created the Kaiyuan Golden Age; later, due to his favoritism toward Consort Yang and reliance on An Lushan, he triggered the An-Shi Rebellion.

In 712, Umayyad general Muhammad ibn al-Qasim completed the conquest of Sindh in the Indus Valley. The Arabs captured the capital of Sindh, Aror, and took the local ruler, King Dahar, prisoner. The Arabs established a province in Sindh, promoted Islam, but allowed Hindus to keep their faith by paying a poll tax (jizya). This conquest brought Islam into the Indian subcontinent.

In July 713, Emperor Xuanzong learned that Princess Taiping planned a coup to depose him and struck first. He sent Wang Maozhong with 300 Forest Army soldiers to kill her allies, Chancellors Xiao Zhizhong and Cen Xi. Princess Taiping fled to a mountain temple but was ordered to commit suicide at home three days later. Xuanzong then fully controlled the court, changed the era name to Kaiyuan, and began to govern diligently. Princess Taiping's faction was wiped out, ending the pattern of female interference in politics that had persisted since Wu Zetian.

v.t: Beginning of the Kaiyuan Golden Age
v.s: In 713, after eliminating Princess Taiping's faction, Emperor Xuanzong changed the era name to Kaiyuan and appointed Yao Chong as chancellor. Yao Chong proposed 'Ten Points', advocating the reform of officialdom, prohibition of extravagance, reduction of taxes, and encouraging free speech. Xuanzong adopted these suggestions and implemented reforms, cutting redundant officials, improving the imperial examinations, building water conservancy projects, and promoting agriculture. The Tang dynasty entered a period of prosperity lasting more than 40 years, known as the Kaiyuan Golden Age, the peak of Tang and Chinese feudal society.

v.t: Emperor Xuanzong Establishes the Jiedushi System
v.s: In 714, Emperor Xuanzong began establishing military commissioners (jiedushi) in border regions, granting them authority over military, civil, and financial affairs within their jurisdictions. Initially, ten jiedushi were established: Anxi, Beiting, Hexi, Longyou, Shuofang, Hedong, Fanyang, Pinglu, Jiannan, and Lingnan. The jiedushi system was intended to improve border defense efficiency, but later led to excessive power of regional military governors, becoming the institutional root of the An-Shi Rebellion and the subsequent藩镇 (fanzhen) separatism.

In 714, the Tang established the Heishui Protectorate in the northeast, appointing the chief of the Heishui Mohe as protector to strengthen control over the Amur River basin. The Heishui Protectorate was part of the Tang's Jimi (loose-rein) system, maintaining frontier order by conferring titles on local chieftains. This measure strengthened ties between the central dynasty and northeastern ethnic groups and promoted regional cultural exchange.

v.t: Yao Chong and Song Jing Serve as Chancellors
v.s: In 715, Emperor Xuanzong appointed Yao Chong as chancellor. Yao was expert in officialdom, cutting redundant offices and reforming finances. Yao was followed by Song Jing, who was upright, selected the capable, and restrained the powerful. Yao Chong and Song Jing were praised as 'Yao and Song', representing the wise chancellors of the early Kaiyuan period. With their assistance, the Tang government became clean, the economy prospered, and national power reached its peak.

In 715, Umayyad forces crossed the Amu Darya and attacked Ferghana, a Tang vassal. The Arab army, allied with Tibet, captured Ferghana's capital and expelled the king appointed by Tang. Tang's Protector General of Anxi, Lü Xiujing, counterattacked but could not fully recover the lost territory. This military clash was part of the struggle between Tang and the Arab Empire for influence in Central Asia, foreshadowing the Battle of Talas more than thirty years later.

v.t: Civil War in the Second Turkic Khaganate
v.s: In 716, Qapaghan Qaghan of the Second Turkic Khaganate was killed while campaigning against the Bayirqu tribe, plunging the khaganate into civil war. Qapaghan's nephew, Kul Tigin, staged a coup, killed Qapaghan's sons, and enthroned his brother as Bilge Qaghan. Bilge Qaghan, together with Kul Tigin and the advisor Tonyukuk, stabilized the situation and maintained peace and marriage relations with Tang. The Second Turkic Khaganate grew strong again under Bilge Qaghan, becoming a major northern threat to Tang.

In 716, Umayyad forces again attacked the Byzantine capital, Constantinople. The Arab navy and army attempted a combined assault from sea and land. Byzantine Emperor Leo III used Greek fire (a liquid incendiary weapon) to destroy the Arab fleet while relying on the strong land walls to resist the land attack. After a year of siege, the Arab army was forced to retreat due to supply shortages and plague. This victory consolidated Byzantium's eastern frontier.

v.t: Yao Chong Dismissed, Song Jing Appointed
v.s: In 717, Emperor Xuanzong dismissed Chancellor Yao Chong and appointed Song Jing as Zhongshu Ling. Yao Chong resigned after being impeached for mishandling a locust plague and for his relatives accepting bribes. Song Jing continued Yao's reform policies. He enforced laws strictly, restrained the powerful, and selected the capable. During Song Jing's tenure, Tang's officialdom became even cleaner, ensuring the continued development of the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

In 717, the Arab army launched a second massive siege of Constantinople. Byzantine Emperor Leo III, allied with the Bulgars, used Greek fire to destroy the Arab fleet. The Arab army suffered heavy losses from supply difficulties and disease and was forced to retreat in 718. This victory allowed Byzantium to retain Anatolia and the Balkans and halted the expansion of Islam into southeastern Europe.

v.t: Tang Legal Reforms under Emperor Xuanzong
v.s: In 718, Emperor Xuanzong commissioned Song Jing and Su Ting to revise the statutes, ordinances, and formulas, compiling the 'Kaiyuan Qianling' in 30 volumes. The new laws simplified the complex regulations of the Wu Zhou period and restored the simple principles of the Zhenguan era. Judicial procedures were reformed, torture was forbidden, and death sentences required three reviews. These legislative activities improved the Tang legal system and ensured long-term social stability.

In 718, the Umayyad Caliphate formed a military alliance with the Tibetan Empire to jointly counter Tang influence in Central Asia. Arab and Tibetan forces attacked Tang vassals in the Western Regions, diverting Tang's Anxi forces. This alliance lasted for decades, severely threatening Tang control over the Western Regions and Hexi Corridor and forcing Tang to invest substantial military resources in the region.

In 719, Emperor Xuanzong established the Youzhou Jiedushi to defend against the Khitan and Xi in the northeast. The Youzhou Jiedushi controlled the Fanyang and Pinglu garrisons and was among the earliest jiedushi. This post was later held by An Lushan, becoming the cradle of the An-Shi Rebellion. The jiedushi system, intended to strengthen border defense, instead planted the seeds for future藩镇 separatism.

In 719, Byzantine Emperor Leo III further promoted iconoclastic policies, ordering the removal of all icons and frescoes from churches. The policy was strongly opposed by the Western Church, represented by Pope Gregory II, and by monasteries. Iconoclasm deepened the rift between Eastern and Western Christianity and set the stage for Charlemagne's coronation and the later Great Schism.

In the early 8th century, the Shailendra dynasty of Java began building the Borobudur Buddhist temple. This massive Buddhist monument consists of about two million blocks of volcanic stone, forming a stepped pyramid with nine platforms, symbolizing the stages of spiritual attainment from the realm of desire to the formless realm. The total length of Borobudur's reliefs is three kilometers, depicting the Jataka tales and Buddhist teachings. It is one of the most magnificent Buddhist structures in Southeast Asia.

v.t: Reign of Empress Gensho in Japan
v.s: In 720, during the reign of Empress Gensho of Japan, the court ordered the compilation of the Nihon Shoki. This work, in 30 volumes and written in classical Chinese, is the oldest official history of Japan. It covers from the mythological age to the reign of Empress Jito, blending legend and fact to establish the divine origin of the emperor. Empress Gensho was the fifth female emperor of Japan and continued to promote the ritsuryō system and state protection of Buddhism.

v.t: Arab Empire Advances into the Caucasus
v.s: In 720, Umayyad forces advanced into the Caucasus, conquering parts of Azerbaijan and Armenia. The Arabs took control of the mountain passes and consolidated their northern frontier. The local Christian kingdoms were forced to submit and pay tribute to the caliph, and some of the population converted to Islam. The Arab northward expansion threatened Byzantine and Khazar interests in the Caucasus.

In 721, Umayyad forces crossed the Pyrenees and attacked Toulouse in southern Francia. Duke Odo of Aquitaine led his forces to meet them and heavily defeated the Arab army at the walls of Toulouse, killing the governor Abd al-Rahman. This was the first major defeat the Arabs suffered in Western Europe since conquering Spain, temporarily halting the expansion of Islam into Francia.

In 721, Emperor Xuanzong ordered large-scale water conservancy projects in the Guanzhong and Henan regions. Jingzhao Yin Li Yuanhong led the construction of the canal and irrigation channels near Chang'an, improving agricultural conditions in the Guanzhong plain. At the same time, the Tang built dikes along the lower Yellow River to control flooding. These water conservancy measures promoted agricultural development and provided the material foundation for the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

v.t: Zhang Yue Reforms the Military System
v.s: In 722, Chancellor Zhang Yue proposed to Emperor Xuanzong that the military system be reformed, abolishing the fubing (territorial militia) system and replacing it with a mercenary system. Due to serious land annexation and the collapse of the equal-field system, the fubing system could no longer be sustained. Zhang Yue recruited 250,000 men as permanent guard troops, exempting them from taxes and corvée. The adoption of the mercenary system professionalized the Tang army and increased its combat effectiveness, but also led to the formation of personal attachments between generals and soldiers, laying the groundwork for the An-Shi Rebellion.

In 722, Emperor Xuanzong issued an edict to expand the scale of imperial examinations, increasing the number of passes in the Mingjing and Jinshi categories. He also stipulated that each prefecture and county must annually select and send candidates to the examinations, prohibiting powerful families from monopolizing the selection process. The Tang imperial examination system reached its peak during the Kaiyuan era, allowing many sons of common landlord families to enter officialdom, breaking the political monopoly of the aristocratic clans and broadening the ruling base.

In 723, Umayyad forces marched east into the Central Asian steppe to attack the Turkic tribal confederation. The Arab cavalry engaged the Karluk tribes and captured towns in the Syr Darya basin. The Turkic nomads were forced to migrate westward, affecting the ethnic distribution of Central Asia. The Arabs established military posts in conquered areas, spread Islam, and consolidated their rule over Transoxiana.

v.t: Tang Emperor Xuanzong Prepares Fengshan Ceremony at Mount Tai
v.s: In 723, Emperor Xuanzong prepared for the fengshan ceremony at Mount Tai, the highest ritual of offering sacrifices to heaven and earth. He ordered Zhang Yue to formulate the rituals, repair the roads, and prepare for three years. This grand ceremony, held in 725, symbolized the political and economic prosperity of the Kaiyuan Golden Age. At the summit of Mount Tai, Xuanzong performed the sacrifice to heaven, proclaiming the peak of Tang power.

In 724, Tang generals Zhang Shougui and Wang Zhongsi led campaigns that decisively defeated the Tibetans and Turks, expanding the frontier by a thousand li. Tang established the Four Garrisons of Anxi in the Western Regions, consolidating control over the northern and southern Tian Shan. In the northeast, Tang forces defeated the Khitan and Xi and established the Songmo Protectorate. Tang territory reached its greatest extent during the Kaiyuan period, stretching from the Korean Peninsula in the east to the Pamir Mountains in the west, Lake Baikal in the north, and Vietnam in the south.

v.t: Internal Divisions in the Umayyad Caliphate
v.s: In 724, after Caliph Hisham succeeded to the Umayyad throne, rebellions broke out across the empire. Yemen, Khurasan, and the Berber tribes rose up against Damascus. Hisham sent forces to suppress them, but this consumed much of the state's resources. The internal divisions weakened the caliphate's control over its frontiers and created conditions for the Abbasid Revolution thirty years later.

v.t: Yi Xing Measures the Earth's Meridian
v.s: In 725, Tang astronomer Yi Xing conducted the first large-scale astronomical survey in Chinese history. He organized personnel to select thirteen observation points on the Henan plain, measuring the shadow length of a gnomon to calculate the length of one degree of the Earth's meridian as about 351 li and 80 bu (approximately 131.3 km). This result came 90 years before the first similar measurement in the West, representing a major achievement in ancient astronomy.

In 725, the Japanese court completed compilation of the Nihon Shoki. Compiled under the direction of Prince Toneri, the 30-volume work is written in classical Chinese and covers history from the age of the gods to the reign of Empress Jito. It blends myth and history to legitimize the divine rule of the emperor. The Nihon Shoki, together with the Kojiki, is one of the oldest histories of Japan and an important source for the study of ancient Japanese history and East Asian cultural exchange.

In 726, Byzantine Emperor Leo III issued a decree formally banning the veneration of icons, ordering the removal of icons and religious images from churches. He believed icon veneration violated the biblical commandment against graven images and was a cause of the empire's decline. This decree triggered intense internal conflict, with monasteries and the populace resisting. Iconoclasm lasted more than a century, severely disrupting social stability and artistic development in Byzantium.

v.t: Wang Zhongsi Defeats Tibetans at Qinghai Lake
v.s: In 726, Tang general Wang Zhongsi heavily defeated Tibetan forces in the Qinghai Lake region. Wang led a night attack on the Tibetan camp, killing thousands and capturing more than 200,000 head of livestock. This victory dealt a severe blow to Tibetan eastward expansion and consolidated Tang control over the Hexi Corridor. Wang Zhongsi was promoted to Hexi Jiedushi for his merits, becoming one of the most outstanding generals of the Kaiyuan era.

In 727, the Tibetan king Tri Detsuktsen led a large force to attack the Hexi region. The Tibetans captured Guazhou (modern Guazhou, Gansu), taking Prefect Tian Renxian prisoner, and cut the Tang's communication route to the Western Regions. Emperor Xuanzong ordered Hexi Jiedushi Wang Junchuo to counterattack. Wang defeated the Tibetan pursuers at the foot of the Qilian Mountains and recovered Guazhou. However, Wang was later killed in battle, leaving the Tang's western frontier in a serious situation.

v.t: Arab Army Attacks Tang's Western Regions
v.s: In 727, Umayyad forces from Khurasan attacked Kashgar and Khotan in the Tang Western Regions. Arab cavalry, in alliance with Tibet, attempted to seize Tang control of the region. The Tang Anxi Protectorate sent forces to meet them, and the two sides fought a seesaw battle in the Pamir Mountains. The Tang eventually repelled the Arabs, but Tang rule in the Western Regions was severely weakened.

In 728, Lombard King Liutprand led forces to attack Ravenna, the Byzantine administrative center in Italy. The Lombards captured the Exarchate of Ravenna, and the Byzantine forces retreated to Rome and Naples. Pope Gregory II appealed to the Frankish kingdom for aid, laying the groundwork for the later Donation of Pepin and the coronation of Charlemagne. The Lombard expansion changed the political landscape of the Italian peninsula.

In 728, Prince Nagaya of Japan was forced to commit suicide after being falsely accused of rebellion. Nagaya, a grandson of Emperor Tenmu and a former Minister of the Left, lost a power struggle with the Fujiwara clan. After Fujiwara no Fuhito's death, his sons continued to persecute Nagaya, leading to the Nagaya Incident. This event marked the further expansion of the Fujiwara family's influence and laid the foundation for the later Fujiwara regency (Sekkan) system.

In 729, Tang and Tibet held a conference at Chiling (modern Riyue Mountain, Qinghai) to demarcate the border. Both sides agreed to use Chiling as the boundary, not to invade each other, and to erect a stele on the border. Tang promised to give Tibet large amounts of silk each year, while Tibet pledged not to attack Tang territory. The Chiling conference temporarily eased military conflict between Tang and Tibet, and the border remained peaceful for nearly a decade.

v.t: Princess Jincheng Promotes Tang-Tibetan Cultural Exchange
v.s: In 729, Princess Jincheng actively promoted cultural exchange between Tang and Tibet. She requested that Tang Xuanzong send Chinese classics such as the Book of Rites, Zuo Zhuan, and Selections of Refined Literature to Tibet, and invited Tang artisans to teach textile, construction, and medical skills. Her activities as a marriage partner enhanced cultural integration between Han and Tibetan peoples and introduced Central Plains culture into Tibetan civilization.

In 730, the Berbers of the Arab province of Al-Andalus launched a large-scale uprising. Berber soldiers, resentful of discriminatory policies by the Arab elite, captured Córdoba with support from Berber tribes in North Africa and massacred the Arab aristocracy. The Umayyad caliphate sent forces to suppress the revolt, and after two years of fierce fighting it was put down. This uprising weakened Arab rule in Spain and set the stage for the later independence of the Emirate of Córdoba (the Umayyad remnant).

In 730, King Dae Muye of Balhae resumed marriage relations with Tang. Previously, Balhae had allied with the Khitan to attack Tang's Liaodong region, straining relations. Dae Muye sent envoys to apologize, and Emperor Xuanzong conferred on him the title of Commander of Balhae, continuing the loose-rein system. Balhae fully modeled its institutions on Tang, becoming a civilized state in Northeast Asia, often called the 'Prosperous State of the Eastern Sea'.

In 731, Gregory III was elected pope. He continued the policy of icon veneration and openly broke with Byzantine Emperor Leo III, who was promoting iconoclasm. Gregory III convened a synod in Rome that declared that those who destroyed icons should be excommunicated. He actively sought support from the Frankish kingdom, strengthening the political alliance between the Roman Church and the Carolingian dynasty.

In 731, the Umayyad Caliphate allied with the Karluk Turkic tribe of Central Asia to jointly counter Tang forces in the Western Regions. The Arabs provided weapons and goods to the Karluks, whose cavalry then helped the Arab army attack the Tang vassals of Ferghana and Kashgar. This alliance strengthened the Arab military position in Central Asia, forcing Tang to reinforce the Four Garrisons of Anxi.

In October 732, Charles Martel, Mayor of the Palace of the Frankish kingdom, led Frankish forces to defeat the Umayyad expeditionary army led by Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi between Poitiers and Tours. The charges of the Arab cavalry were stopped by Frankish infantry squares, and Abd al-Rahman was killed in the battle. The Arab army retreated to Spain. This battle halted the expansion of Islam into the heart of Europe, and Charles Martel earned the nickname 'the Hammer'. His son Pepin later founded the Carolingian dynasty.

v.t: Li Bai Leaves Chang'an and Wanders
v.s: In 732, Li Bai, frustrated in Chang'an, left the capital and began a decade of wandering. He had been summoned to serve in the Hanlin Academy as a Hanlin academician, but his uninhibited personality offended the powerful, and he was dismissed with a gift of gold. After leaving Chang'an, Li Bai traveled extensively, meeting Du Fu in Luoyang and composing many great poems in the Liang-Song and Qi-Zhao regions. His poetry reached its creative peak during this period, leaving such masterpieces as 'Hard Is the Way to Shu', 'Hard Is the Way of the World', and 'Bringing in the Wine'.

v.t: Tang Reorganizes Ten Circuits into Fifteen
v.s: In 733, Emperor Xuanzong adjusted the national administrative division from ten circuits to fifteen, each with an inspector responsible for overseeing prefectural officials. The new fifteen circuits were: Jingji, Duji, Guannei, Henan, Hedong, Hebei, Longyou, Shannan East, Shannan West, Jiannan, Huainan, Jiangnan East, Jiangnan West, Qianzhong, and Lingnan. This administrative adjustment strengthened local oversight but also laid the groundwork for later藩镇 separatism.

In 733, Frankish Mayor of the Palace Charles Martel defeated the resistance of Duke Odo of Aquitaine and the Burgundian nobles, completing the unification of the Frankish kingdom. He centralized power, reformed the military, and implemented the benefice system, granting land to knights in exchange for military service. These reforms strengthened the cavalry and laid the foundation for the later Carolingian Empire.

In 734, Emperor Xuanzong established the Anxi Jiedushi, uniting military forces of the Anxi Protectorate and Beiting Protectorate. The Anxi Jiedushi was responsible for defending against Tibetan and Arab expansion eastward and had jurisdiction over the southern Tianshan route and the Pamir region. The first Anxi Jiedushi was Fu Menglingzhao, and his subordinate general Gao Xianzhi later became the most famous Tang commander in the Western Regions.

In 734, Li Linfu was appointed chancellor by Emperor Xuanzong. Li was skilled at gauging Xuanzong's will, suppressing dissent, and consolidating imperial power. He excluded wise chancellors like Zhang Jiuling and promoted a policy of 'no talent left in the countryside', blocking the path of examination candidates into officialdom. Li Linfu monopolized power for nineteen years and was a key figure in Tang's decline from prosperity to decay. His tenure saw increasing political corruption, sowing the seeds for the An-Shi Rebellion.

v.t: Saint Boniface Establishes Bishoprics in Germany
v.s: In 735, with the support of Charles Martel, Saint Boniface established several bishoprics in Germany, including Mainz, Passau, and Regensburg. He reformed discipline in the German church, abolished pagan customs, and spread Christianity widely among the Germanic peoples. Boniface was later revered as the 'Apostle to the Germans'. His missionary work promoted the Christianization of the Frankish kingdom and the civilization of Germanic tribes.

v.t: Gao Xianzhi's First Expedition to Little Balhae
v.s: In 735, Tang Anxi general Gao Xianzhi led an expedition against Little Balhae (in modern Gilgit, Kashmir). The king of Little Balhae had submitted to Tibet, cutting the Silk Road route from Tang to the Western Regions. Gao Xianzhi led 10,000 elite troops across the Pamir Mountains, surprising and capturing the Little Balhae capital and its king. This expedition allowed Tang to regain control of the vital communications route to Central Asia, and Gao Xianzhi's fame spread throughout the Western Regions.

In 736, Frankish Mayor of the Palace Charles Martel led a campaign against the Saxons. The Saxons, a Germanic tribe still adhering to paganism, frequently raided the eastern borders of the Frankish kingdom. Charles Martel heavily defeated the Saxon army on the Weser River, forcing them to submit and pay tribute. This victory consolidated Frankish control over the region east of the Rhine and laid the groundwork for Charlemagne's later complete conquest of Saxony.

In 736, Emperor Xuanzong dismissed Chancellor Zhang Jiuling and allowed Li Linfu to dominate court affairs. Zhang had opposed deposing Crown Prince Li Ying and relying on An Lushan, leading to conflict with Li Linfu. Li Linfu slandered Zhang to Xuanzong, and Zhang was demoted to be the governor of Jingzhou. Zhang Jiuling was the last virtuous chancellor of the Kaiyuan Golden Age; his dismissal marked the beginning of political decay and decline in the Tang dynasty.

In 737, war broke out between Tang and the Turgesh Khaganate in Central Asia. The Turgesh khagan Suluk, allied with Tibet, attacked Tang positions in the Western Regions. Tang Anxi Jiedushi Gai Jiayun led his forces against them. The two sides fought a fierce battle around Suyab (modern Tokmok, Kyrgyzstan), and the Tang army eventually repelled the Turgesh, stabilizing the situation in the Western Regions. The following year, civil war broke out in the Turgesh khaganate, Suluk was killed, and Tang regained suzerainty over Central Asia.

v.t: Li Linfu Persecutes Crown Prince Li Ying
v.s: In 737, Li Linfu, in collusion with Consort Wu, falsely accused Crown Prince Li Ying, Prince Li Yao of E, and Prince Li Ju of Guang of plotting rebellion. Emperor Xuanzong believed the slander and on a single day reduced the three princes to commoner status and ordered them to commit suicide. After eliminating the crown prince's faction, Li Linfu supported Consort Wu's son, Prince Li Mao of Shou, as crown prince. However, Xuanzong ultimately followed Gao Lishi's advice and made Prince Li Heng (later Emperor Suzong) crown prince. The conflict between Li Linfu and the crown prince later evolved into a prolonged political struggle.

In 738, Piluoge, chief of the Mengshe tribe, united the six zhao with Tang support and established the Nanzhao kingdom. Emperor Xuanzong of Tang granted Piluoge the title 'Prince of Yunnan', and he established his capital at Taihe City (modern Dali, Yunnan). Nanzhao adopted Tang institutions, promoted Chinese culture, but maintained military independence. The establishment of Nanzhao changed the political landscape of the southwest and later became a major threat to Tang in that region.

v.t: Arab Empire Completes Conquest of Central Asia
v.s: In 738, Umayyad forces finally conquered all of Transoxiana, wiping out the last resisting Sogdian city-states. The Arabs established garrisons and administrative offices in Samarkand, Bukhara, and Ferghana, and forcibly promoted Islam. Many Sogdians converted to Islam and adopted Arabic, beginning the long process of Islamization and Turkification of Central Asia.

v.t: Height of the Maya Kingdom of Copán
v.s: In the mid-8th century, Maya civilization entered its Late Classic period, and the kingdom of Copán reached its peak. Copán, located in modern western Honduras, was one of the most important Maya city-states. King Uaxaclajuun Ubʼaah Kʼawiil (r. 695–738) built numerous pyramids, altars, and stelae. Copán's Hieroglyphic Stairway is the longest carved Maya inscription. In 738, the king was captured and executed by the rival city-state of Quiriguá, and Copán entered a period of decline.

v.t: Emperor Shōmu of Japan Orders Construction of Tōdai-ji
v.s: In 739, Emperor Shōmu of Japan issued an edict to build Tōdai-ji temple in Heijō-kyō (Nara). Tōdai-ji is the head temple of the Japanese Kegon school of Buddhism. Its main hall, the Daibutsuden, is one of the largest wooden structures in the world. Inside is a bronze statue of the Great Buddha (Vairocana), about 15 meters tall, which consumed the nation's copper resources. The construction of Tōdai-ji reflected the Nara period's policy of making Buddhism a state religion, but it also consumed enormous resources and labor.

In 739, An Lushan, known for his military prowess and flattery, gradually gained the trust of Emperor Xuanzong. He bribed court officials and eunuchs to speak well of him. An Lushan was successively appointed as Pinglu Jiedushi and Fanyang Jiedushi, gaining control over military and political power in the northeast. He raised a large number of non-Han generals and elite troops and secretly plotted rebellion, planting the seeds for the An-Shi Rebellion sixteen years later.

In 740, the great Tang landscape poet Meng Haoran died of illness at the age of 51. Meng Haoran was a pioneer of landscape poetry in the Tang, often mentioned together with Wang Wei as 'Wang-Meng'. His poetry is fresh and natural, with a serene and remote mood. Representative works include 'Spring Morning', 'Visiting an Old Friend's Cottage', and 'Mooring on the River at Jiande'. Meng Haoran remained a commoner his whole life, refusing to serve the powerful, and spent his later years in seclusion in Lumen Mountain. His poetic style had a profound influence on later Chinese poetry.

v.t: Pepin the Short Becomes Frankish Mayor of the Palace
v.s: In 740, after the death of Frankish Mayor of the Palace Charles Martel, his son Pepin III (Pepin the Short) inherited the office. Pepin the Short co-ruled with his brother Carloman. Ambitious, he sought to seize the throne. Pepin actively sought the support of the Pope, preparing to depose the last Merovingian king and establish the Carolingian dynasty.

In October 741, Frankish Mayor of the Palace Charles Martel died of illness. Charles Martel was the de facto ruler of the Franks at the end of the Merovingian period. He defeated the Arab army at the Battle of Tours, preventing the expansion of Islam into the heart of Europe. His benefice reforms strengthened cavalry and laid the foundation for the Carolingian Empire. After his death, his sons Pepin the Short and Carloman inherited power.

In 741, Tibetan forces captured the key Tang fortress of Shibao (in modern Huangyuan, Qinghai). Shibao Fortress, located on the Tang-Tibetan border, was perched on a cliff and very difficult to capture. After occupying it, the Tibetans used it as a base to constantly raid the Hexi Corridor. Emperor Xuanzong ordered Geshu Han to retake the fortress, but Geshu Han hesitated, unwilling to launch a costly assault. It was not until 749 that Tang forces recaptured it at great cost.

v.t: Emperor Xuanzong Changes Era Name to Tianbao
v.s: In the first month of 742, Emperor Xuanzong changed the era name to Tianbao, marking the beginning of the later phase of his reign. Xuanzong was now 57 years old, began to tire of state affairs, and entrusted government to Chancellor Li Linfu while indulging in pleasure. The Tianbao era saw increasing political corruption, severe land annexation, and the growth of藩镇 military power, all of which sowed the seeds for the An-Shi Rebellion. The Tianbao era lasted fourteen years, a critical period of Tang's decline from prosperity to decay.

In 742, Emperor Xuanzong appointed An Lushan as Pinglu Jiedushi, responsible for defending against the Khitan and Xi in the northeast. An Lushan was of mixed barbarian ancestry, brave in battle, and skilled at winning people's hearts. Using his position as jiedushi, he raised elite troops, cultivated ties with non-Han generals, and built up his power. Later he also became Fanyang Jiedushi and Hedong Jiedushi, thereby controlling the most elite frontier forces of the Tang and becoming the largest military power in the empire.

In 742, after the death of Byzantine Emperor Constantine V, iconoclasm was temporarily suspended. Constantine V, son of Leo III, had continued iconoclastic policies, cruelly persecuting monasteries and icon venerators. His death gave hope to the iconophile party, but iconoclasm was not truly ended until the Second Council of Nicaea in 787, which formally restored the veneration of icons.

v.t: Casting of the Great Buddha at Tōdai-ji Begins
v.s: In 743, Emperor Shōmu of Japan ordered the casting of the Great Buddha at Tōdai-ji. The casting project took nearly a decade, consuming the nation's copper resources and huge amounts of labor and wealth. The Buddha is about 15 meters tall and resides in the Great Buddha Hall of Tōdai-ji. Emperor Shōmu hoped to pray for peace and protection through this act, but the enormous cost aggravated social contradictions and accelerated the decline of the ritsuryō state.

v.t: An Lushan Visits Court and Flatters
v.s: In 743, An Lushan came to court to see Emperor Xuanzong. He flattered Xuanzong profusely, acknowledged Consort Yang as his mother, and each time he entered court he would first pay respects to Consort Yang and then to Xuanzong. When asked why, he replied, 'Barbarians honor mother before father.' Xuanzong was delighted and trusted An Lushan even more. An Lushan seized the opportunity to request also being appointed Fanyang Jiedushi, which was granted, further expanding his military power.

In 744, the Uyghur chief Qutlugh Bilge Köl (Guli Peiluo) unified the nine Uyghur tribes and established the Uyghur Khaganate, proclaiming himself Qutlugh Bilge Köl Qaghan. Emperor Xuanzong granted him the title 'Huai Ren Khagan', recognizing the legitimacy of the Uyghur Khaganate. The Uyghur Khaganate maintained marriage alliances and trade relations with Tang and assisted Tang in suppressing rebellions. The Uyghur Khaganate lasted nearly a century, controlling the Mongolian Plateau and the northern Western Regions, and was an important northern ally of Tang.

v.t: Li Bai Summoned to Court as Hanlin Academician
v.s: In 744, Li Bai was summoned to the capital by Emperor Xuanzong to serve as a Hanlin academician, a literary attendant to the emperor responsible for drafting edicts and accompanying him on excursions. In the palace, Li Bai wrote three poems under the tune 'Qingping Diao', praising the beauty of Consort Yang. However, his uninhibited personality offended Gao Lishi and Yang Guozhong, and less than two years later, he was dismissed with a gift of gold. This summons was Li Bai's last experience in government.

v.t: Pepin the Short Unifies the Franks
v.s: In 744, Pepin the Short expelled his brother Carloman and unified the Frankish kingdom. Carloman was forced to retire to a monastery, and Pepin became the sole Mayor of the Palace. He actively sought the Pope's support to depose the last Merovingian king, Childeric III, and proclaim himself king. Pepin's unification paved the way for the establishment of the Carolingian dynasty.

v.t: Consort Yang Enters the Palace and Is Enfeoffed
v.s: In 745, Emperor Xuanzong conferred the title of Imperial Consort (Guifei) on Yang Yuhuan, who had previously been the consort of Prince Shou. Consort Yang was naturally beautiful and skilled in music, deeply loved by Xuanzong. To please her, Xuanzong spent enormous sums to transport fresh lychees from Lingnan to Chang'an, inspiring the famous line 'The red dust of a single ride raised a lady's smile.' After Consort Yang gained favor, her cousin Yang Guozhong and her three sisters all received high positions and generous stipends, and the Yang family became extremely powerful, provoking discontent at court and among the people.

v.t: Li Bai and Du Fu Become Friends
v.s: In 745, Li Bai and Du Fu met in Luoyang and formed a deep friendship. They traveled together in the Liang-Song region (modern Kaifeng and Shangqiu, Henan), seeking Taoist teachings and composing poems while drinking. Du Fu wrote in a poem: 'Drunk, we slept with our robes overlapping; hand in hand, we traveled by day.' Li Bai was eleven years older than Du Fu, and their friendship became a celebrated story in Tang literary circles. Du Fu later wrote many poems expressing his admiration for Li Bai.

v.t: Tang Territory Reaches Its Greatest Extent
v.s: In 746, the Tang dynasty's territory reached its largest historical extent: east to the Taedong River basin in Korea, west to the Pamir Mountains and the Aral Sea, north to Lake Baikal, and south to the Heng Mountains in Vietnam. Tang established six protectorates (Anxi, Beiting, Anbei, Danyu, Andong, Annan) in the frontier regions, forming an imperial system characterized by loose-rein (jimi) governance. The directly ruled area of Tang was about 10 million square kilometers, making it one of the largest empires in the world at the time.

v.t: Li Linfu Persecutes Wei Jian and Huangfu Weiming
v.s: In 746, Chancellor Li Linfu falsely accused the Minister of Revenue Wei Jian and Longyou Jiedushi Huangfu Weiming of plotting rebellion and attempting to depose Crown Prince Li Heng. Wei and Huangfu were demoted and killed, and Crown Prince Li Heng was forced to divorce his consort (Wei's sister) to protect himself. Li Linfu used the opportunity to attack his political enemies, implicating dozens of families in this first major Tianbao-era injustice. Crown Prince Li Heng became extremely cautious and dared not act until after Li Linfu's death.

v.t: Gao Xianzhi's Expedition to Little Balhae
v.s: In 747, Tang Anxi Deputy Protector Gao Xianzhi led 10,000 elite troops on an expedition to Little Balhae. He crossed the Pamir Mountains, which are over 4,000 meters high, and surprised the Little Balhae capital, capturing the king and reopening the Silk Road. Emperor Xuanzong promoted Gao Xianzhi to Anxi Jiedushi and granted him the title of Duke of Miyun. Gao's expedition demonstrated Tang military superiority in the Western Regions but also aroused the vigilance of the Arab Empire.

v.t: An Lushan's Power Overwhelms the Court
v.s: In 747, An Lushan came to court to present captives, and for his military achievements Emperor Xuanzong promoted him to Prince of Dongping. This was the first time a Tang jiedushi had been enfeoffed as a prince, a sign of the court's loss of control over regional military governors. An Lushan then requested and was granted the concurrent position of Hedong Jiedushi. He now held three jiedushi positions (Pinglu, Fanyang, Hedong) simultaneously, commanding 150,000 troops, the most elite frontier forces of Tang. The seeds of the An-Shi Rebellion had already sprouted.

In 748, Frankish Mayor of the Palace Pepin the Short convened an assembly of nobles at Soissons and was elected king. He deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III and established the Carolingian dynasty. Pope Zachary recognized Pepin's usurpation, stating that 'the one who actually wields power should be king.' Pepin's coronation set a precedent for papal coronation of kings and established a political model of mutual support between ecclesiastical and royal power.

In 748, Emperor Xuanzong appointed Geshu Han as Longyou Jiedushi, responsible for defense against Tibet. Geshu Han was a Turk known for his bravery. After taking office, he actively trained troops and prepared to recapture Shibao Fortress from the Tibetans. Geshu Han's governance strengthened Tang's western frontier defense, but he was politically attached to Chancellor Li Linfu and participated in schemes against the crown prince.

In 749, Longyou Jiedushi Geshu Han led 60,000 Tang troops to besiege Shibao Fortress. The fortress was perched on a cliff, defended by only a few hundred Tibetan soldiers, but its position was extremely strong. After several days of heavy assault, the Tang suffered tens of thousands of casualties before finally breaching the walls and capturing the Tibetan commander. Emperor Xuanzong ordered the fortress renamed Shenwu Army and promoted Geshu Han to Tèjin and Chief of the Court of Diplomatic Reception. Although victorious, the heavy losses reflected the declining combat effectiveness of the Tang army during the Tianbao era.

v.t: Paper-Making Reaches Samarkand in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 749, the Arab Empire established its first paper mill in Samarkand. Paper-making technology was brought to the Arab world by captured Chinese craftsmen after the Battle of Talas. Samarkand became the center of paper production in the Islamic world. Paper replaced parchment and papyrus, greatly promoting the Arab translation movement and having a profound impact on the Golden Age of Islamic civilization.

In 750, Abu al-Abbas defeated the Umayyad army at the Battle of the Great Zab River in Iraq and established the Abbasid Caliphate. The Abbasids moved the capital from Damascus east to Baghdad, marking the shift of the center of Islamic civilization from Syria to Mesopotamia. Under caliphs such as Harun al-Rashid and al-Ma'mun, the Abbasid Caliphate entered the Islamic Golden Age. The House of Wisdom in Baghdad gathered scholars of various ethnicities and achieved remarkable accomplishments in astronomy, mathematics, medicine, and philosophy.

v.t: Early Recipes for Gunpowder Recorded in China
v.s: Around 750, Tang alchemists first recorded early recipes for gunpowder. Daoist alchemical texts such as the 'Zhenyuan Miao Dao Yao Lüe' described the combustion of mixtures of sulfur, saltpeter, and charcoal. Although not yet used militarily at the time, this discovery laid the foundation for later gunpowder weapons. Gunpowder is one of the Four Great Inventions of ancient China and had a revolutionary impact on the course of world civilization.

v.t: Ghana Empire at Its Peak in West Africa
v.s: In 750, the Ghana Empire of West Africa entered its peak period. The Ghana Empire controlled West African gold production and the trans-Saharan trade routes, and its kings accumulated great wealth through monopolizing the gold trade. The capital, Koumbi Saleh, was divided into a Muslim merchant quarter and a native residential quarter. The Ghana Empire remained strong until the 11th century and was the first great empire in West African civilization.

In July 751, Tang Anxi Jiedushi Gao Xianzhi led 30,000 Tang troops against an Abbasid army of 200,000 led by Abu Muslim at Talas (modern Taraz, Kazakhstan). The Tang army initially gained the upper hand against the combined force of Arabs and Karluk tribes, but the Karluks defected midway, causing the Tang army to collapse. Gao Xianzhi escaped back to Anxi with only a few thousand men. This battle led to Tang losing control over Central Asia, while the Arab Empire consolidated its rule there. Captured Tang craftsmen introduced paper-making to the Arab world.

v.t: Paper-Making Transmitted to the Islamic World
v.s: After the Battle of Talas in 751, captured Tang paper-making craftsmen brought the technology to Samarkand. The Arabs quickly mastered paper-making and established paper mills in Baghdad, Damascus, and Cairo. The spread of paper greatly promoted the Arab translation movement, allowing Greek, Indian, and Persian scientific works to be preserved and disseminated. Paper-making then spread via North Africa to Spain and Italy, playing an important role in the European Renaissance.

In 752, An Lushan secretly built up forces in Fanyang (modern Beijing) preparing for rebellion. In Fanyang, he raised 8,000 elite cavalry from the Tongluo, Xi, and Khitan tribes, called 'Yeluohe'. He also stockpiled grain, armor, and weapons, and recruited non-Han generals and outlaws. His strategists Gao Shang and Yan Zhuang actively planned the rebellion, but Emperor Xuanzong remained confident of An Lushan's loyalty.

In 752, after Chancellor Li Linfu died of illness, Yang Guozhong, cousin of Consort Yang, succeeded him as chancellor. Yang Guozhong, originally named Yang Zhao, was renamed by Xuanzong. He acted autocratically, excluded dissent, and his conflict with An Lushan grew increasingly acute. During Yang Guozhong's tenure, political corruption worsened, finances became increasingly difficult, and the Tang ruling crisis deepened. He repeatedly reported to Xuanzong that An Lushan was plotting rebellion, but Xuanzong did not take it seriously.

v.t: Nanzhao Breaks with Tang and Allies with Tibet
v.s: In 752, King Geluofeng of Nanzhao, resentful of mistreatment by the Tang frontier general Zhang Qiantuo, rose in rebellion against Tang. Geluofeng captured Yaozhou (modern Yao'an, Yunnan) and killed Zhang Qiantuo. Tang sent Xianyu Zhongtong with 80,000 troops to suppress the rebellion, but they were heavily defeated before the Nanzhao capital of Taihe City. Nanzhao then turned to Tibet, and the Tibetan emperor granted Geluofeng the title 'Zanputong' (Brother of the Emperor). The break between Nanzhao and Tang changed the political landscape of the southwest.

In 753, after leaving Chang'an, Li Bai began his later wanderings. He traveled through Liang-Song, Qi-Lu, and Wu-Yue, exchanging verses with friends such as Du Fu and Gao Shi. During these travels, Li Bai wrote many poems expressing disappointment with reality and longing for freedom. His poetry reached its artistic peak during this period, and such masterpieces as 'Mount Skyland Ascended in a Dream' and 'Farewell to a Secretary at the Xie Tiao Tower in Xuanzhou' were written at this time.

v.t: Geshu Han and An Lushan Compete for Power
v.s: In 753, the conflict between Longyou Jiedushi Geshu Han and An Lushan intensified. The two attacked each other at court, and Geshu Han once publicly exposed signs of An Lushan's rebellion. Emperor Xuanzong tried to mediate by granting Geshu Han the title of Prince of Xiping and giving him high office and generous stipends. However, Geshu Han suffered a stroke that left him partially paralyzed, and he was unable to effectively restrain An Lushan, whose preparations for rebellion became increasingly brazen.

In 754, the Tang monk Jianzhen finally succeeded in his sixth attempt to cross to Japan. Jianzhen had failed five times since 743, and had become blind, but he never gave up. He brought Buddhist scriptures, statues, and medical texts to Japan, where he was warmly received by Empress Kōken and Emperor Shōmu (retired). In Japan, Jianzhen taught the precepts, built Tōshōdai-ji temple, and promoted the development of Japanese Buddhism and Sino-Japanese cultural exchange.

In 754, Tang sent Li Mi with 100,000 troops to attack Nanzhao, attempting to recover Yaozhou. King Geluofeng of Nanzhao, allied with Tibetan forces, gave battle to the Tang army before Taihe City. The Tang army, deep in Nanzhao territory, suffered supply difficulties and disease in the miasmal climate, leading to massive deaths. Li Mi was defeated and committed suicide, and the Tang army was annihilated. In this battle, Tang lost 100,000 elite troops, crippling its national power and leaving it unable to suppress An Lushan's rebellion.

On the ninth day of the eleventh month of 755, An Lushan, who concurrently held the three jiedushi positions of Fanyang, Pinglu, and Hedong, raised a rebellion in Fanyang (modern Beijing) under the pretext of receiving a secret edict to punish Yang Guozhong. An Lushan led 150,000 rebel troops southward, meeting little resistance. Emperor Xuanzong hurriedly ordered Feng Changqing and Gao Xianzhi to defend, but Luoyang soon fell. In the twelfth month, An Lushan proclaimed himself emperor of the Great Yan dynasty in Luoyang. The An-Shi Rebellion was the turning point of Tang's decline from prosperity to decay and lasted eight years.

In December 755, An Lushan's rebel forces captured the eastern capital, Luoyang. The Tang generals Feng Changqing and Gao Xianzhi retreated to Tong Pass, but were falsely accused by the eunuch Bian Lingcheng and executed. Emperor Xuanzong activated the ailing Geshu Han to defend Tong Pass. An Lushan proclaimed himself emperor of the Great Yan dynasty in Luoyang, changing the era name to Shengwu. The fall of Luoyang caused the Tang to lose its eastern capital, and the court was shaken, with the situation critical.

v.t: Mawei Coup and Death of Consort Yang
v.s: In June 756, An Lushan's rebel forces broke through Tong Pass, and Geshu Han was defeated and captured. Emperor Xuanzong fled in panic to Sichuan. At Mawei Station (modern Xingping, Shaanxi), the imperial guards mutinied, killing Chancellor Yang Guozhong and forcing Xuanzong to order Consort Yang strangled. Xuanzong reluctantly ordered Gao Lishi to strangle Consort Yang, who was 38 years old. Xuanzong then fled to Chengdu, while Crown Prince Li Heng went north to Lingwu to organize resistance.

v.t: Emperor Suzong Ascends the Throne in Lingwu
v.s: In July 756, Crown Prince Li Heng ascended the throne in Lingwu (modern Lingwu, Ningxia) as Emperor Suzong, honoring Xuanzong as retired emperor. Suzong appointed Guo Ziyi as Shuofang Jiedushi and Li Guangbi as Hedong Jiedushi, and mobilized northwestern frontier troops to suppress the rebellion. He also sought troops from the Uyghur Khaganate, agreeing that after the rebellion was put down, 'the land and people shall return to Tang, but the gold, silk, and women shall go to the Uyghurs.' Suzong's ascension stabilized the leadership core of the counter-rebellion effort.

In the first month of 756, An Lushan proclaimed himself emperor in Luoyang, with the dynastic name Great Yan and era name Shengwu. He appointed his son An Qingxu as Prince of Jin, An Qingen as Prince of Zheng, and Gao Shang and Yan Zhuang as chancellors. After becoming emperor, An Lushan became increasingly cruel and paranoid, alienating his subordinates, leading to internal contradictions. The following year, An Lushan was killed in a plot by his son An Qingxu and the advisor Yan Zhuang.

In September 757, Guo Ziyi led a combined Tang and Uyghur force of 150,000 to a decisive battle against the rebels at Xiangji Temple on the western outskirts of Chang'an. The Tang forces killed over 60,000 rebels and recaptured Chang'an. When Emperor Suzong entered the city, 'the people cheered, their voices shaking heaven and earth.' In October, Tang forces also recaptured Luoyang, and An Qingxu fled to Yecheng. The recapture of Chang'an was a major turning point in the rebellion, giving Tang the initiative.

v.t: An Lushan Killed, An Qingxu Succeeds
v.s: In the first month of 757, An Lushan was killed in a plot by his son An Qingxu and the eunuch Li Zhu'er, as An Qingxu feared that An Lushan's favoritism toward his youngest son would endanger his own position. An Qingxu succeeded as emperor of Great Yan, with the era name Zaichu. An Qingxu was incompetent and given to pleasure, and power fell into the hands of the powerful minister Yan Zhuang. The rebels fell into internal chaos, their combat effectiveness greatly reduced, and Tang took the opportunity to launch a counteroffensive.

In September 758, Emperor Suzong ordered nine jiedushi led by Guo Ziyi and Li Guangbi to invest Yecheng (modern Anyang, Henan), where An Qingxu was holed up. The Tang forces besieged the city for months, digging moats and damming rivers to flood it. The rebels were trapped and awaiting relief. However, because Suzong distrusted his generals, he did not appoint a supreme commander, and the various commanders were not subordinate to each other, leading to a protracted siege. In March of the following year, Shi Siming arrived with relief forces, and the Tang army was badly defeated, and the siege collapsed.

In October 758, Guo Ziyi led Tang forces to recapture Luoyang. An Qingxu retreated to Yecheng. After retaking the two capitals, Emperor Suzong rewarded his generals generously, making Guo Ziyi Duke of Dai. However, Suzong was deeply suspicious of military men like Guo Ziyi and sent the eunuch Yu Chao'en to supervise the army, interfering in military command. This marked the beginning of eunuch domination, a chronic malady of the late Tang.

In March 759, after defeating the Tang army at Yecheng, Shi Siming tricked and killed An Qingxu, absorbed his forces, and proclaimed himself emperor of Great Yan. Shi Siming, a former subordinate of An Lushan, was far more militarily capable than An Qingxu. He led the rebel forces south and recaptured Luoyang, drastically worsening Tang's counter-rebellion situation. Shi Siming slaughtered Tang prisoners of war in Luoyang to intimidate the court and populace.

In September 759, Shi Siming led rebel forces to attack Heyang (modern Mengzhou, Henan). Tang general Li Guangbi defended Heyang with 20,000 troops, facing 100,000 rebel troops. Li Guangbi used a ruse to inflict a major defeat on the rebels, killing over 10,000 and stabilizing the defense line north of Luoyang. The Battle of Heyang was an important victory in the counter-rebellion effort, preventing the rebels from advancing northward to attack Guanzhong.

v.t: Du Fu Writes 'Three Officials' and 'Three Farewells'
v.s: In 760, during the chaos of the An-Shi Rebellion, Du Fu composed the famous historical poems 'Three Officials' ('Recruiting Officer at Xinyi', 'Officer at Shihao', 'Officer at Tong Pass') and 'Three Farewells' ('Farewell to Newlyweds', 'Farewell of an Old Man', 'Farewell of a Homeless Man'). These poems vividly record the suffering brought by the war to the common people, reflecting the social reality of Tang's decline from prosperity to decay. Du Fu was later revered as the 'Sage of Poetry', and his poetry as 'history in verse'.

In 760, Li Bai died of illness in Dangtu, Anhui, at the age of 59. In his later years, Li Bai had been exiled to Yelang for joining the staff of Prince Li Lin, but was pardoned on the way. In his last poem, 'Singing on His Deathbed', he lamented his unfulfilled ambitions. Li Bai was the greatest romantic poet of the Tang dynasty, with over 900 extant poems. His poetry, with its splendid imagination and vigorous language, had a profound influence on later literature.

In 760, the Rashtrakuta dynasty rose on the Deccan Plateau in India. King Dantidurga defeated the Chalukya dynasty and established an empire. The Rashtrakutas made Ellora their capital and carved the famous Kailasa Temple, a monolithic rock-cut Hindu temple that is a masterpiece of Indian architecture. The Rashtrakuta Empire maintained trade and cultural links with the Abbasid Caliphate in Baghdad, promoting Indian Ocean trade.

v.t: Shi Siming Killed by His Son
v.s: In March 761, Shi Siming was killed by his son Shi Chaoyi. Shi Siming favored his youngest son Shi Chaoqing and wanted to depose the eldest and establish the youngest, so Shi Chaoyi preemptively killed his father and succeeded as emperor of Great Yan. Shi Chaoyi was incompetent, surrounded himself with eunuchs, and the rebel forces further fragmented. During Shi Chaoyi's rule, the rebel forces gradually disintegrated, and the Tang counter-rebellion entered its final phase.

In November 761, Tang forces recaptured Luoyang. Shi Chaoyi fled to Fanyang. With the help of Uyghur cavalry, the Tang entered Luoyang, but the Uyghurs plundered the city, killing thousands of civilians, and Luoyang was severely devastated. Although Tang had recaptured its eastern capital, the nation's power was greatly diminished, and it could never recover the prosperity of the Kaiyuan Golden Age.

In April 762, Emperor Suzong died of illness, and Crown Prince Li Yu ascended the throne as Emperor Daizong. After taking the throne, Daizong continued to suppress the An-Shi Rebellion. He employed the eunuchs Li Fuguo and Cheng Yuanzhen in power, inaugurating the pattern of eunuch domination in the Tang dynasty. During Daizong's reign, the An-Shi Rebellion was finally pacified, but problems such as藩镇 separatism and Tibetan invasions became increasingly severe.

In 762, Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur built a new capital, Baghdad, on the west bank of the Tigris River. Baghdad was circular in shape, about two kilometers in diameter, with four gates, and was known as the 'Round City'. Baghdad quickly became the political, economic, and cultural center of the Islamic world, with a population of over one million, one of the largest cities in the world at the time. The House of Wisdom in Baghdad gathered scholars of various ethnicities, and studies in astronomy, medicine, mathematics, and philosophy reached their zenith.

In the first month of 763, Shi Chaoyi, defeated and cornered, committed suicide in Fanyang. The eight-year An-Shi Rebellion was finally pacified. The Tang counter-rebellion effort came at a heavy cost: the population fell from about 52 million before the war to 17 million; the economy was severely damaged; a藩镇 separatist pattern emerged; and Tibet took advantage to capture the Hexi Corridor. The An-Shi Rebellion was the turning point of Tang's decline, after which Tang fell into a pattern of藩镇 separatism and eunuch domination.

In October 763, the Tibetan Emperor Trisong Detsen led 200,000 troops into Guanzhong, catching Tang unprepared. Emperor Daizong fled in haste to Shanzhou (modern Sanmenxia, Henan). The Tibetan forces captured Chang'an and installed Li Chenghong as emperor. They plundered the city for fifteen days. Guo Ziyi led a counterattack, and the Tibetans withdrew from Chang'an. This was the first time the Tang capital was captured by a foreign power, marking the collapse of Tang's western frontier defenses.

In 764, the Tang general Pugu Huai'en, having been falsely accused by the eunuch Cheng Yuanzhen, raised a rebellion in Hedong. Pugu Huai'en had rendered great service in suppressing the An-Shi Rebellion and had been made Grand Guardian. He allied with Tibetan and Uyghur forces and repeatedly raided Guanzhong. Emperor Daizong brought back Guo Ziyi, who used his personal prestige to break up Pugu Huai'en's alliance. Pugu Huai'en died of illness in 765, and the rebellion was suppressed.

In 764, Emperor Daizong gave his daughter, Princess Ningguo, in marriage to the Uyghur khagan Bögü Qaghan. Tang hoped through this marriage alliance to solidify its alliance with the Uyghurs and use Uyghur cavalry to counter Tibet and the藩镇. The Uyghurs, in turn, obtained silk and trade privileges from Tang. This marriage relationship maintained peace between Tang and the Uyghurs for about forty years.

v.t: Guo Ziyi Rides Alone to Persuade the Uyghurs
v.s: In 765, Pugu Huai'en united Tibetan, Uyghur, and Tangut forces totaling 300,000 to invade Guanzhong. Guo Ziyi led Tang forces to defend Jingyang. Guo Ziyi rode alone into the Uyghur camp, reasoning with them and persuading them to ally with Tang. The Uyghurs turned their coats and, together with Tang forces, attacked the Tibetans, who fled in defeat. Guo Ziyi's heroic act of outsmarting the three armies stabilized Tang's northwestern frontier.

In 765, Tibetan forces completely occupied the Hexi Corridor, cutting Tang's communication with the Western Regions. Liangzhou (modern Wuwei, Gansu), Ganzhou (modern Zhangye), and Suzhou (modern Jiuquan) fell one after another. The Tang Anxi Protectorate and Beiting Protectorate became isolated exclaves, cut off from the central government. The loss of the Hexi Corridor deprived Tang of its important horse-breeding base, and the horse administration collapsed.

v.t: Emperor Daizong Changes Era Name to Dali
v.s: In 766, Emperor Daizong changed the era name to Dali, beginning the use of the Dali era name. During the Dali era, Tang faced internal and external troubles: constant Tibetan invasions, worsening藩镇 separatism, and the arrogance and tyranny of eunuchs Li Fuguo and Cheng Yuanzhen. Daizong was unable to solve these problems and could only maintain a precarious rule. The Dali era lasted fourteen years, a transitional period from disorder to a degree of order.

v.t: Empress Shōtoku Ascends the Throne in Japan
v.s: In 766, Empress Shōtoku ascended the throne in Japan. Empress Shōtoku was the title taken by the former Empress Kōken after her abdication. She had a controversial relationship with the monk Dōkyō, causing discontent at court. During Empress Shōtoku's reign, Dōkyō interfered in politics and attempted to usurp the throne. After her death, Dōkyō was exiled. During this period, the Japanese court was in turmoil, and the ritsuryō state faced crisis.

In 767, Du Fu died of illness in Leiyang, Hunan, at the age of 58. In his later years, Du Fu wandered in the Hunan and Hubei area, living in poverty. Before his death, he wrote poems such as 'On My Deathbed' lamenting his unfulfilled ambitions. Du Fu left over 1,400 poems, which profoundly reflect the social reality of Tang's decline and have earned him the title 'Sage of Poetry'. Du Fu, together with Bai Juyi and Li Bai, are known as the three great poets of the Tang.

In 767, the Hebei藩镇 leaders Li Baochen, Li Zhengji, and Tian Chengsi, each with their own armies, defied court orders. They intermarried and formed political alliances, ruling their regions as separate states. Emperor Daizong tried to reduce their power but was unable to send troops against them and could only recognize their hereditary succession. The Hebei藩镇 became states within the Tang state, where the court's orders did not apply and taxes were not remitted.

v.t: Charlemagne Becomes King of the Franks
v.s: In September 768, after the death of Frankish King Pepin the Short, the kingdom was divided between his two sons, Charles and Carloman, according to Frankish tradition. Charles's territory included Neustria, Aquitaine, and western Austrasia; Carloman ruled Burgundy, Alsace, and Provence. The brothers were on bad terms. Charles seized Carloman's territory after Carloman's death in 771, unifying the Frankish kingdom.

In 768, the great Tang poet Bai Juyi was born in Xinzheng, Zhengzhou (modern Xinzheng, Henan). Bai Juyi, courtesy name Letian, art name Xiangshan Jushi, was a leading proponent of the New Yuefu Movement in the Tang, advocating that 'writing should serve the times, and poetry should address reality.' His representative works include 'Song of Everlasting Sorrow', 'Song of the Pipa', and 'The Old Charcoal Seller'. Bai Juyi's poetry is plain and accessible, widely circulated, and had a profound influence on later Chinese literature.

In 769, Charlemagne led an expedition to Aquitaine, suppressing a rebellion. The Aquitanian duke Hunald fled to Gascony. Charlemagne then conquered Gascony, pushing the southern border of the Frankish kingdom to the Pyrenees. This campaign consolidated Charlemagne's rule over western Francia and laid the groundwork for the later conquests of Lombardy and Spain.

In 769, Emperor Daizong, a devout Buddhist, ordered a large number of ordinations across the country. He set annual quotas for ordinations in each prefecture and bestowed large amounts of land on monasteries. Daizong often held Buddhist services in the palace and discussed Chan Buddhism with monks. This pro-Buddhist policy increased the state's financial burden, diverted labor from production, and accelerated the decline of the Tang economy.

Around 770, Hindu-Arabic numerals (0-9) were introduced to the Iberian Peninsula via Córdoba in Spain. The Arabs had adopted the decimal place-value system from India and perfected it during the Abbasid period. Christian European scholars encountered this mathematical system through the translation centers of Spain, laying the foundation for the later revival of mathematics in Europe and the commercial revolution.

In the mid-to-late 8th century, Abbasid caliphs al-Mansur and Harun al-Rashid strongly supported the translation movement in Baghdad. Scholars at the House of Wisdom translated Greek, Persian, and Indian philosophical, medical, mathematical, and astronomical works into Arabic. The works of Aristotle, Plato, Euclid, Ptolemy, and Galen were preserved. This translation movement lasted about two hundred years and preserved the flame of classical civilization.

v.t: Formation of the Three Hebei藩镇
v.s: In 770, the Hebei region saw the formation of the three major藩镇: Chengde, Weibo, and Lulong. Chengde jiedushi Li Baochen, Weibo jiedushi Tian Chengsi, and Lulong jiedushi Li Huaixian each commanded tens of thousands of troops, did not remit taxes to the court, and passed their positions from father to son. The court was unable to send forces against them and had to adopt a policy of appeasement.藩镇 separatism became a chronic political problem of the late Tang, persisting until the dynasty's fall.

In 771, Charlemagne, at the request of Pope Hadrian I, led an army against the Lombard kingdom. The Lombard king Desiderius refused to return papal territories. Charlemagne led Frankish forces across the Alps and laid siege to the Lombard capital, Pavia. After seven months of siege, Pavia fell, and Charlemagne crowned himself King of the Lombards. This conquest gave Charlemagne control of northern Italy and cemented his alliance with the Pope.

In December 771, Charlemagne's brother Carloman died, and Charlemagne seized Carloman's territory, unifying the Frankish kingdom. He became the sole king of the Franks, with a vast domain from the Atlantic to the Danube. Charlemagne then began large-scale expansion, conquering Bavaria, Saxony, and the Spanish March, and building the vast Carolingian Empire.

In 772, Charlemagne launched his first major campaign against the Saxons. The Saxons, a Germanic tribe still practicing pagan religion, frequently raided the Frankish borders. Charlemagne led his forces to destroy the Irminsul, a sacred pillar of the Saxons, and forced the Saxons to convert to Christianity. The Saxon Wars lasted more than thirty years and were the longest and bloodiest of Charlemagne's reign.

In 772, Bai Juyi was only four years old but already showed extraordinary literary talent. From a young age he was exceptionally bright; he learned to write poetry at five or six, and mastered rhyme at nine. Despite his family's poverty, Bai Juyi studied so hard that his mouth became sore and his elbows calloused. These early experiences laid the foundation for his later becoming one of the greatest poets of the Tang dynasty.

In 773, Charlemagne completed the conquest of the Lombard kingdom. The last Lombard king, Desiderius, was captured and exiled to a monastery. Charlemagne proclaimed himself King of the Lombards and incorporated northern Italy into the Frankish realm.
