He retained Lombard laws and administrative systems but appointed Frankish nobles to key positions. This conquest freed the Pope from the Lombard threat and consolidated the papal state after the Donation of Pepin.

In 773, the Tibetan Emperor Trisong Detsen led a large army to attack Tang's Longyou region. The Tibetans captured Qinzhou (modern Tianshui) and Weizhou (modern Longxi), threatening Chang'an. Emperor Daizong urgently ordered Guo Ziyi to lead the defense. Guo Ziyi engaged the Tibetans at Lingtai (modern Lingtai, Shaanxi), repulsing them and temporarily easing the crisis in Guanzhong.

In 774, Charlemagne conquered the Duchy of Bavaria. Duke Tassilo III of Bavaria, a cousin of Charlemagne, was deposed and sent to a monastery for plotting independence. Charlemagne incorporated Bavaria into the Frankish kingdom and established margraves to rule it. The incorporation of Bavaria pushed the Frankish eastern frontier to the Danube, bringing it into contact with the Avar Khaganate.

In 774, Chancellor Yuan Zai dominated the court during Emperor Daizong's reign. Yuan Zai, of humble origin, rose to the chancellorship through the patronage of Li Fuguo. He took bribes, sold offices, and allowed his sons and relatives to act with impunity. Daizong grew increasingly dissatisfied but tolerated him because of his many supporters. Only in 777 did Daizong put Yuan Zai in prison and order him to commit suicide.

In 775, the Srivijaya Empire in Southeast Asia (located on modern Sumatra, Indonesia) reached its peak. Srivijaya controlled the Strait of Malacca trade, becoming a hub of the maritime Silk Road between East and West. The empire followed Mahayana Buddhism and was a center of Buddhism in Southeast Asia. Srivijaya maintained close trade relations with Tang, India, and the Arab Empire, amassing great wealth.

In 775, Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur established the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, the earliest academic research institution in the Islamic world. The House of Wisdom gathered scholars of various ethnicities for translation and research. An observatory, library, and medical school were established there, and Baghdad replaced Alexandria as the academic center of the world. The Islamic Golden Age began.

In 775, the Saxons, led by Duke Widukind, launched a large-scale rebellion. They destroyed churches built by Charlemagne and massacred Frankish garrisons and missionaries. Charlemagne led his army against them, defeating the Saxons on the Weser River, but could not fully suppress the rebellion. The Saxon Wars continued until 785, when Charlemagne executed 4,500 Saxon prisoners and forced the Saxons to submit.

In 776, Charlemagne held a grand Easter celebration in Rome to celebrate the conquest of Lombardy. Pope Hadrian I crowned Charlemagne 'Patrician of the Romans'. This celebration marked the consolidation of the alliance between the Frankish kingdom and the Papacy, and reflected Charlemagne's ambition to restore the glory of the Western Roman Empire. Twenty-four years later, Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne 'Emperor of the Romans' on Christmas Day.

In 776, Emperor Daizong ordered the casting of Dali Tongbao coins. After the An-Shi Rebellion, the Tang economy declined, copper coins were scarce, and prices soared. Daizong attempted to stabilize the economy by minting new coins, but the Dali Tongbao were crudely cast and underweight, and the populace refused to use them. The failure of this monetary reform reflected the dire financial straits of the Tang and its declining ability to manage the economy.

In 777, Charlemagne, at the request of Sulayman al-Arabi, the Muslim governor of Zaragoza, led an expedition to Spain. Sulayman, who had fallen out with the Emir of Córdoba, asked Charlemagne for help. The Frankish army crossed the Pyrenees and laid siege to Zaragoza. But Sulayman suddenly changed his stance, and Charlemagne was forced to retreat. During the retreat, Basques ambushed the Frankish rearguard at Roncesvalles, and Count Roland was killed, an event immortalized in the 'Song of Roland'.

In March 777, Emperor Daizong ordered the arrest of Chancellor Yuan Zai and ordered him to commit suicide. A huge amount of embezzled goods was found at Yuan Zai's residence, and his associates were all demoted or killed. Daizong appointed Yang Wan as chancellor, hoping to reform governance. But Yang Wan soon died of illness, and Daizong appointed Chang Gun as chancellor. Chang Gun was honest but incompetent, and the corruption of Daizong's court did not improve.

In 778, Charlemagne again led an expedition to Spain, capturing Pamplona and occupying the Basque region. He established the Spanish March as a buffer against the Emir of Córdoba. The Spanish March later developed into the Kingdom of Navarre and the Kingdom of Aragon. Although Charlemagne's Spanish expedition did not conquer the entire peninsula, it established a solid defensive line for the Carolingian Empire on its southwestern frontier.

v.t: War Among the Three Hebei藩镇
v.s: In 778, war broke out between Chengde jiedushi Li Baochen, Weibo jiedushi Tian Chengsi, and Lulong jiedushi Li Huaixian. The three藩镇 attacked each other, fighting over territory and population. The court was powerless to stop them and could only look on. The internecine warfare among the藩镇 devastated the Hebei region's economy, causing people to flee and production to collapse.

In May 779, Emperor Daizong died of illness, and Crown Prince Li Kuo ascended the throne as Emperor Dezong. After taking the throne, Dezong was determined to reduce the power of the藩镇 and restore central authority. He appointed Yang Yan as chancellor, implemented the Two-Tax system, and reformed finances. In the early part of his reign, Dezong was diligent and ambitious, but after suffering several藩镇 rebellions he lost confidence and instead favored eunuchs.

In 779, the Saxons rebelled once more. Charlemagne used brutal means to suppress them, executing 4,500 Saxon prisoners at Verden in one go and forcing the Saxons to convert to Christianity en masse. This atrocity provoked even greater Saxon resistance, but eventually, in 785, the Saxon leader Widukind surrendered and accepted baptism. Saxony was incorporated into the Frankish kingdom.

In 780, Chancellor Yang Yan proposed to Emperor Dezong that the equal-field and rent-adjustment (zu-yong-diao) system be abolished and replaced by the Two-Tax system. The Two-Tax system collected household tax and land tax instead of the former taxes, levied in summer and autumn, with rates based on assets and land area. The Two-Tax system simplified the tax structure, increased government revenue, and recognized private land ownership, marking a major reform in Chinese tax history. However, it also increased the burden on peasants and widened the gap between rich and poor.

In 780, Charlemagne issued the 'Capitulary of Saxony', forcing the Saxons to convert to Christianity. The capitulary stipulated: death for destroying a church, death for refusing baptism, death for eating meat during Lent. Charlemagne used harsh laws to promote Christianization while also strengthening church control over society. These measures fully Christianized the Saxon region but also provoked long-term resistance.

In 781, the Nestorian Stele was erected at the Daqin Temple (Nestorian church) in Chang'an. The inscription, written by the Persian missionary Jingjing, details the history of Nestorian Christianity in China. It mentions that in the ninth year of Zhenguan (635), Nestorianism arrived in Chang'an, and Emperor Taizong ordered the construction of a church. The stele is an important artifact for studying the history of Christianity in China and Sino-foreign cultural exchange.

In 781, Chengde jiedushi Li Baochen died, and his son Li Weiyue requested to inherit his father's position. Emperor Dezong resolutely refused and ordered a campaign against Chengde. Li Weiyue, together with Weibo's Tian Yue, Ziqing's Li Na, and Lulong's Zhu Tao, rose in rebellion. Dezong sent forces to suppress them, but the藩镇 supported each other, and the imperial forces suffered repeated defeats. Dezong's ambition to reduce藩镇 power was severely frustrated.

In 782, Charlemagne ordered the execution of 4,500 Saxon prisoners to avenge the Saxon rebellion. This event, which took place at Verden (modern Verden, Germany), was the largest single massacre during Charlemagne's conquests. Charlemagne hoped to break Saxon resistance through terror. However, the massacre only provoked even fiercer resistance, and it was not until 785 that Saxony finally submitted.

In October 782, the Jingyuan army in Chang'an mutinied over unfair treatment. The soldiers stormed the imperial palace, looted the treasury, and supported Zhu Ci as emperor. Emperor Dezong fled in panic to Fengtian (modern Qianxian, Shaanxi). Zhu Ci laid siege to Fengtian, and Dezong appealed to various jiedushi for help. The following year, Li Sheng led forces to recapture Chang'an, and Dezong was able to return. The Jingyuan Mutiny was a major political crisis of Dezong's reign, after which Dezong lost trust in military commanders and began to favor eunuchs.

v.t: Zhu Ci Proclaims Himself Emperor, Fengtian Besieged
v.s: In 783, Zhu Ci proclaimed himself emperor in Chang'an, with the dynastic name Great Qin. He sent troops to besiege Fengtian, and Emperor Dezong was trapped in the city. Zhu Ci's rebels attacked day and night, and Fengtian was nearly lost. At the critical moment, Shuofang jiedushi Li Huaiguang arrived with relief forces, defeated Zhu Ci, and saved Dezong. But Li Huaiguang became arrogant and fell out with the court, and the following year he too rebelled.

In 783, Charlemagne led an expedition against the Avar Khaganate. The Avars, a nomadic people from the Eurasian steppe, had repeatedly raided the Frankish eastern frontier. Charlemagne's eldest son, Charles the Younger, led the vanguard and captured the Avars' ring-shaped camp, seizing the enormous wealth the Avars had accumulated over years of plunder. This expedition completely destroyed the Avar Khaganate, and its territory was incorporated into the Carolingian Empire.

In May 784, Tang general Li Sheng led forces to recapture Chang'an. He decisively defeated Zhu Ci's rebels outside the city, and Zhu Ci was killed while fleeing to Jingzhou. In June, Emperor Dezong returned to Chang'an and rewarded Li Sheng and others for their merits. However, after this upheaval, Dezong lost trust in military commanders and began to favor eunuchs controlling the Shence Army, inaugurating an era of eunuch domination in the Tang dynasty.

In 784, Shuofang jiedushi Li Huaiguang, resentful of court suspicion, rose in rebellion. Li Huaiguang had rendered great service in relieving Fengtian, but Dezong refused to see him and sent eunuchs to supervise his army. Li Huaiguang was forced to retreat to Hezhong (modern Yongji, Shanxi) and oppose the court. Dezong ordered Ma Sui and Hun Zhen to campaign against him, and Li Huaiguang was defeated and committed suicide in 786.

In April 785, Tang and Tibet held a treaty conference at Qingshui (modern Qingshui, Gansu). The two sides demarcated the border: Tibet retained the Hexi and Longyou regions, while Tang controlled Guanzhong and Bashu. Tang recognized Tibet's occupation of the Hexi Corridor, and Tibet promised not to invade Tang again. The Qingshui Treaty was an important agreement in Tang-Tibetan relations, marking Tang's formal abandonment of control over the Western Regions.

In 785, the Saxon leader Widukind surrendered to Charlemagne and accepted baptism at Attigny, taking the name 'Christian' (meaning Christian). Charlemagne pardoned Widukind and appointed him Duke of Saxony. Saxony was thus fully incorporated into the Frankish kingdom. Charlemagne established churches and monasteries in Saxony, forcibly promoted Christianization, and Saxony was fully brought into the Carolingian cultural orbit.

v.t: Emperor Kammu of Japan Moves Capital to Nagaoka-kyō
v.s: In 786, Emperor Kammu of Japan moved the capital from Heijō-kyō (Nara) to Nagaoka-kyō (near modern Kyoto). The powerful Buddhist monasteries in Heijō-kyō had interfered in politics, and Kammu hoped to escape their influence by moving the capital. The construction of Nagaoka-kyō consumed enormous resources and labor, but ten years later, due to flooding and other problems, Kammu moved the capital again to Heian-kyō (modern Kyoto).

v.t: Li Baozhen Suppresses Li Na's Rebellion
v.s: In 786, Zhaoyi jiedushi Li Baozhen led forces to suppress the rebellion of Ziqing jiedushi Li Na. Li Na had inherited the position from his father Li Zhengji and ruled Shandong as an independent fief. Li Baozhen decisively defeated Li Na at Xuzhou, forcing him to submit to the court. Li Baozhen was one of the few jiedushi loyal to the court during Dezong's reign, and his actions stabilized the situation in the southeast.

In 787, Tang and Tibet held a treaty conference at Pingliang (modern Pingliang, Gansu). The Tibetans laid an ambush, captured the Tang envoy Hun Zhen, and killed more than sixty Tang officials. Hun Zhen escaped alone, while the captured officials were all killed. The Pingliang Betrayal was the most serious diplomatic incident in Tang-Tibetan relations, and afterwards the two sides returned to a state of war. Tang's western border was in constant turmoil for a long time.

In 787, Byzantine Empress Irene convened the Seventh Ecumenical Council at Nicaea. The council condemned iconoclasm and restored the veneration of icons. It ruled that icons could be venerated but not worshipped as gods. The council ended the iconoclastic controversy that had lasted about sixty years in Byzantium, but also deepened theological differences with the Frankish kingdom.

In 788, Emperor Dezong appointed Wei Gao as Jiedushi of Jiannan Xichuan. Wei Gao governed the Shu region for nearly twenty years, reorganizing the military, developing the economy, and strengthening border defenses. He repeatedly repelled invasions by Tibet and Nanzhao, becoming one of the most outstanding frontier generals of Dezong's reign. Under Wei Gao's governance, Shu became one of the richest regions in the late Tang, providing an important source of revenue for the court.

In 788, the Abbasid Caliphate and the Byzantine Empire continued to fight along the Anatolian frontier. Arab forces advanced deep into Anatolia, capturing some Byzantine towns. Byzantine Empress Irene tried to negotiate peace with the caliphate but failed. The seesaw war on the Anatolian frontier lasted for decades and severely consumed Byzantine resources.

In 789, King Yimouxun of Nanzhao, unable to bear Tibetan oppression, voluntarily sought peace with Tang. He sent envoys carrying gold ore as a token of sincerity, hoping to restore the alliance with Tang. Tang's Jiannan Jiedushi Wei Gao formed an alliance with Nanzhao to jointly counter Tibet. Nanzhao's return to Tang placed Tibet in a pincer movement, severely weakening Tibetan military power.

In 789, Charlemagne issued the 'Capitulary de Villis', regulating the management of royal estates. The capitulary detailed production, financial, and judicial systems for estates, requiring officials at all levels to perform their duties diligently. Charlemagne also sent missi dominici (royal envoys) to tour the provinces and supervise local administration. These reforms strengthened central authority, improved governance efficiency, and had a profound impact on the European medieval feudal system.

Around 790, the Viking navigator Naddodd was blown off course while sailing to the Faroe Islands and discovered Iceland. He named the island 'Snæland' (Snowland). Later, the Vikings Garðar Svavarsson and Flóki Vilgerðarson also reached Iceland. The climate of Iceland was cold but suitable for grazing, providing the Vikings with a new colony.

At the end of the 8th century, Vikings began large-scale raids on European coastal areas. They attacked English monasteries, Irish coastal towns, and Frankish ports. Using the mobility of their longships, they came and went like the wind, raiding and quickly withdrawing. These raids marked the beginning of the Viking Age, and for the next two hundred years, Vikings would ravage much of Europe.

v.t: Fall of Tang's Western Regions Protectorates
v.s: In 790, Tibetan forces captured Kucha (modern Kuche, Xinjiang), the seat of the Tang Anxi Protectorate. The last Tang stronghold in the Western Regions fell, and Tang completely lost control over the region. Thereafter, the Western Regions fell into the hands of Tibet and the Uyghurs, and were not reunified under Chinese rule until the Qing dynasty a thousand years later.

In 791, Charlemagne established a series of marches (military frontier zones) along the empire's borders. These marches were governed by margraves with considerable military autonomy, responsible for defending against external enemies. Important marches included the Danish March (against the Danes), the Spanish March (against Córdoba), and the Avar March (against the Avars). The march system strengthened border defense but also laid the foundation for later regional separatism.

v.t: Wei Gao, Allied with Nanzhao, Crushes Tibet
v.s: In 791, Jiannan Jiedushi Wei Gao, in alliance with Nanzhao king Yimouxun, launched an attack on Tibet. The Tang-Nanzhao allied forces decisively defeated the Tibetan army on the Lushui River, killing the Tibetan general and capturing tens of thousands. This victory severely damaged Tibetan military power and weakened Tibetan control over the southwestern region. Wei Gao was promoted to Prince of Nankang for his merits.

v.t: Defects of the Two-Tax System Appear
v.s: In 792, defects in the Two-Tax system gradually emerged. Local officials, seeking to curry favor, inflated household numbers, increasing the burden on peasants. Merchants and landowners evaded taxes through various means, and tax inequity worsened. Coupled with successive natural disasters, peasants went bankrupt and fled, and Tang finances again fell into difficulty. Dezong tried to reform the system but failed.

v.t: Bai Juyi Passes the Jinshi Examination
v.s: In 792, Bai Juyi passed the jinshi examination at the age of 24. There was a saying in the Tang: 'At thirty you are old for the Mingjing, at fifty you are young for the Jinshi.' Bai's youth when passing the exam shows his exceptional talent. After passing, he wrote a poem: 'Among the seventeen whose names are inscribed below the Ci'en Pagoda, I am the youngest.' Passing the jinshi laid the foundation for Bai Juyi's official career.

In June 793, Viking pirates attacked Lindisfarne Priory on the northeast coast of England. The Vikings killed the monks, looted the monastery's treasures, and burned the buildings. This attack is regarded as the beginning of the Viking Age. A contemporary English chronicler wrote: 'Never before has such terror appeared in Britain.' After that, Vikings raided England every year.

In 793, Tang and the Uyghur Khaganate began large-scale silk-horse trade. Tang purchased tens of thousands of horses from the Uyghurs each year, exchanging 40 bolts of silk per horse. The Uyghurs made huge profits from the trade, while Tang suffered heavy losses of silk each year, burdening the treasury. But to maintain the military alliance with the Uyghurs, Tang had to continue this unequal trade.

v.t: Japan Moves Capital to Heian-kyō, Beginning Heian Period
v.s: In 794, Emperor Kammu of Japan moved the capital from Nagaoka-kyō to Heian-kyō (modern Kyoto). Heian-kyō was designed as a grid pattern imitating Tang Chang'an, covering about 25 square kilometers. The move marked the beginning of the Heian period (794–1185) in Japanese history. The Heian period was the heyday of Japanese aristocratic culture, producing literary masterpieces such as 'The Tale of Genji' and 'The Pillow Book'.

In 794, Jiannan Jiedushi Wei Gao heavily defeated Tibetan forces at Weizhou (modern Li County, Sichuan). Wei Gao followed up the victory, recovering some prefectures occupied by Tibet. The Tibetan emperor Trisong Detsen was forced to negotiate peace with Tang. Wei Gao's 21-year governance of Shu ensured long-term stability on Tang's southwestern frontier.

In 795, Charlemagne continued his campaign against the Avar Khaganate. Frankish forces captured the Avars' ring-shaped camp and seized enormous wealth. The treasures were transported to Aachen and used to build palaces and churches. The Avar Khaganate was completely destroyed, and the remaining Avars were assimilated into neighboring peoples. Charlemagne incorporated the middle Danube plain into the empire.

In 795, Jiannan Jiedushi Wei Gao supported the spread of Nestorian Christianity in Shu. He built the Daqin Temple in Chengdu and allowed Persian missionaries to preach openly. Nestorianism developed to some extent in the mid-to-late Tang, with believers including some non-Han and Han Chinese. Wei Gao's support for Nestorianism reflects Tang's tolerant attitude toward foreign cultures.

In 796, Charlemagne's son Pepin the Hunchback led forces to completely defeat the Avars, capturing their last fortress. The Avar Khaganate was destroyed, and the remaining Avars were forced to accept Frankish rule. Charlemagne distributed the Avar wealth to the church and nobles, and established the Pannonian March there.

In 796, Emperor Dezong appointed the eunuchs Dou Wenchang and Huo Xianming as commanders of the left and right Shence armies, establishing the precedent of eunuchs controlling the imperial guard. After the Jingyuan Mutiny, Dezong no longer trusted military commanders and entrusted military authority to eunuchs. Eunuch domination became a chronic political problem of the late Tang, with even the appointment and deposition of emperors decided by eunuchs, lasting until the dynasty's fall.

v.t: Empress Irene Becomes Sole Ruler of Byzantium
v.s: In 797, Byzantine Empress Irene deposed and blinded her son Constantine VI, becoming the sole ruler of the Byzantine Empire. Irene was the first woman to rule the Byzantine Empire in her own right. She restored the veneration of icons and actively sought good relations with the Frankish kingdom and the Arab Empire. Her reign sparked a debate over female succession, providing a pretext for Charlemagne's coronation as 'Emperor of the Romans'.

v.t: Heian Aristocratic Culture Emerges in Japan
v.s: At the end of the 8th century, Japan's Heian aristocratic culture began to take shape. The aristocrats prized elegance and refinement, with emphasis on etiquette and aesthetics. Literary genres such as waka poetry, monogatari, and nikki flourished, and kana script gradually became widespread. Aristocratic women played a significant role in literary creation, with writers such as Sei Shōnagon and Murasaki Shikibu later producing 'The Pillow Book' and 'The Tale of Genji'.

In 797, Charlemagne built a palace and cathedral in Aachen (modern Aachen, Germany). Aachen became the political and cultural center of the Carolingian Empire. At his court, Charlemagne gathered the finest scholars in Europe, such as Alcuin and Einhard, sparking the 'Carolingian Renaissance'. The palace architecture at Aachen imitated Byzantine styles from Ravenna, reflecting Charlemagne's ambition to restore the Roman imperial tradition.

In 798, the Abbasid Caliphate suppressed the rebellion of Rafi ibn al-Layth in Central Asia. The rebels had captured Samarkand, gaining support from the local Sogdian population. Caliph Harun al-Rashid sent forces to put down the rebellion, and it took two years of fierce fighting to recapture Samarkand. This rebellion revealed the instability of Abbasid rule in Central Asia, after which the caliphate gradually lost effective control over the region.

v.t: Han Yu Passes the Jinshi Examination
v.s: In 798, the Tang literary figure Han Yu passed the jinshi examination. Han Yu, courtesy name Tuizhi, was a native of Heyang (modern Mengzhou, Henan). He was a proponent of the Classical Prose Movement, opposing parallel prose and advocating a return to the prose style of pre-Qin and Han dynasties. Han Yu was later revered as the foremost of the 'Eight Great Prose Masters of the Tang and Song', and his essays had a profound influence on the Neo-Confucianism of the Song and Ming dynasties.

In April 799, Pope Leo III was attacked by his enemies in Rome and thrown into prison. Leo III escaped and fled to Charlemagne at Paderborn to seek help. Charlemagne sent envoys to escort Leo III back to Rome and decided to go to Rome himself to handle the matter. The following year, Charlemagne convened a synod in Rome and restored Leo III to the papacy. In return, Leo III crowned Charlemagne 'Emperor of the Romans' on Christmas Day.

In 799, Bai Juyi was appointed a collator (xiaoshu lang), beginning his official career. The collator was a position in the Palace Library responsible for collating texts. During his tenure in Chang'an, Bai Juyi befriended Yuan Zhen and others, together advocating the New Yuefu Movement. He wrote poems such as 'Qin Zhongyin' and 'New Yuefu' that reflected social reality, embodying his principle that 'writing should serve the times, and poetry should address reality.'

On Christmas Day in the year 800, Pope Leo III crowned Charlemagne as 'Emperor of the Romans' in St. Peter's Basilica in Rome. Charlemagne became the first emperor to be crowned by a pope since the fall of the Western Roman Empire, marking the reconstruction of political order in Western Europe. The Carolingian Empire claimed to be the successor of the Roman Empire, standing alongside the Byzantine Empire in the East. Charlemagne's coronation was a major event in medieval European history, establishing the medieval relationship between pope and emperor.

v.t: Bai Juyi Composes 'Song of Everlasting Sorrow'
v.s: Around 800, Bai Juyi wrote the long narrative poem 'Song of Everlasting Sorrow'. The poem, 120 lines in length, tells the tragic love story of Emperor Xuanzong and Consort Yang. From 'The beauty-loving monarch longed to topple a kingdom' to 'Their eternal sorrow would never end', the language is beautiful and emotions deep. This poem is one of the most famous narrative poems in Chinese literary history, widely circulated and having a profound influence on later drama and fiction.

v.t: Han Yu Initiates the Classical Prose Movement
v.s: Around 800, Han Yu initiated the Classical Prose Movement in the literary world, opposing the parallel prose style that had prevailed since the Six Dynasties. He advocated that 'writing should convey the Way', that essays should elucidate the Confucian orthodoxy and serve social reality. Han Yu's representative works such as 'On Teachers', 'Explanation of Learning', and 'The Origin of the Way' are plain, fluent, and persuasive. The Classical Prose Movement received support from Liu Zongyuan and others, having a transformative impact on the development of Chinese prose.

v.t: Ghana Empire Flourishes in West Africa
v.s: In 800, the Ghana Empire of West Africa entered its period of florescence. The Ghana Empire controlled West African gold production and the trans-Saharan trade routes, and its kings accumulated vast wealth through monopolizing the gold and salt trade. The capital, Koumbi Saleh, was divided into a Muslim merchant quarter and a native residential quarter. The Ghana Empire was the first great empire in West African history and had a profound influence on the later Mali and Songhai empires.

Around 800, Polynesian navigators (ancestors of the Māori) discovered New Zealand, traveling in outrigger canoes from the Cook or Society Islands. They reached the North Island of New Zealand. The Māori settled in New Zealand, developing a unique culture and becoming the indigenous people of the land.

v.t: Swahili City-States Emerge on Southeast African Coast
v.s: Around 800–1500, the Swahili people of the East African coast established a series of trading city-states, including Kilwa, Malindi, Mombasa, and Zanzibar. These city-states blended Bantu African culture with Islamic civilization, using the Swahili language (Bantu + Arabic loanwords) and stone architecture (coral stone). Swahili merchants controlled Indian Ocean trade, trading gold, ivory, slaves, and porcelain with Arabia, India, and China. The Great Mosque of Kilwa was one of the largest stone buildings in East Africa at the time.

v.t: Hopewell Culture of North American Woodland Period
v.s: Roughly 200 BCE – 500 CE, the Hopewell culture (in modern Ohio) of the Eastern Woodlands region built large geometric earthworks as burial and ceremonial sites. The Hopewell established a vast trade network stretching from the Rockies to Florida, and from the Great Lakes to the Gulf of Mexico. They traded exotic materials such as obsidian, mica, shark teeth, sea turtle shells, and copper. The Hopewell culture declined mysteriously around 500 CE and was succeeded by the Mississippian culture.
